inclusion_criteria: Comparative clinical studies (randomized controlled trials, controlled clinical trials, or prospective/retrospective cohort studies) that include a no-interceptive or delayed-treatment control group.
Participants are children aged 10–13 years in the mixed dentition with clinically and/or radiographically diagnosed palatally displaced maxillary permanent canines.
Intervention is extraction of the primary maxillary canine as the sole interceptive procedure (passive space maintenance allowed).

Report outcomes on normalization of eruptive position and/or successful spontaneous eruption of the palatally displaced maxillary canine, assessed clinically or radiographically, with follow-up sufficient to observe eruption.
exclusion_criteria: Non-comparative designs (case reports/series), reviews, editorials, conference abstracts, or non-human studies.

Populations with craniofacial anomalies/syndromes, cleft lip/palate, relevant systemic conditions, prior comprehensive orthodontic treatment affecting canine eruption, or cases with buccally displaced (non-palatal) canines; outside 10–13 years or not in mixed dentition.

Interventions combining primary canine extraction with additional active orthodontic or surgical procedures during the interceptive phase (e.g., rapid maxillary expansion, distalization, surgical exposure with orthodontic traction).
Studies that do not report eruption-related outcomes (normalization/successful eruption) or do not provide separate data for palatally displaced canines and/or the target age group.
screening_criteria.description: Screening criteria to identify comparative clinical studies in 10–13-year-old mixed-dentition children with palatally displaced maxillary canines evaluating interceptive extraction of the primary canine versus no or delayed treatment, with outcomes on eruption normalization/success.

included_articles.title: Two interceptive approaches to palatally displaced canines: a prospective longitudinal study.
Extraction of the deciduous canine as an interceptive treatment in children with palatal displaced canines - part I: shall we extract the deciduous canine or not?
A randomized clinical study of two interceptive approaches to palatally displaced canines.
The use of panoramic radiographs to decide when interceptive extraction is beneficial in children with palatally displaced canines based on a randomized clinical trial.
Effect of interceptive extraction of deciduous canine on palatally displaced maxillary canine: a prospective randomized controlled study.
